text:
Jocelyn Noveck

Associated Press

Published:July 31, 2024 at 12:31 PM

Updated:August 1, 2024 at 1:11 PM

Recommended Videos

Jocelyn Noveck

Associated Press

Copyright 2024 The Associated Press. All rights reserved

Hugo Bardin who performs as the drag queen Paloma speaks, during an interview with The Associated Press in Paris, France, Wednesday, July 31, 2024. (AP Photo/Michel Euler)

PARISвАУ As a gay youth growing up in central France, Hugo Bardin never felt he lived in a world that represented who he was вАФ a world in which he had a place.

And that is why Bardin, who performs as the drag queen Paloma, felt it was meaningful and important to be part ofa Paris Olympics opening ceremonythat presented a multifaceted, multiethnic France with people of different ethnicities and orientations.

Recommended Videos

вАЬIt was a really important moment for the French people and the representation of France around the world,вАЭ says Paloma, who took part in a single scene that has drawn some furious criticism вАФ including from presidential candidate Donald Trump in the United States, who called it вАЬa disgrace.вАЭ

Although the ceremonyвАЩsartistic director, Thomas Jolly, and other participants have repeatedly said the scene wasnвАЩt inspired by вАЬThe Last Supper,вАЭcritics interpreted that part of the showas a mockery of Leonardo Da VinciвАЩs painting showing Jesus Christ and his apostles.

Paloma, best known for winning вАЬDrag Race France,вАЭ appeared with other drag artists and dancers alongside Barbara Butch, a popular DJ who wore a silver headdress that looked like a halo. Butch has nowfiled a complaint alleging online abuse and harassment,and Paris police have launched an investigation.

Paloma is not, at this point, planning to take legal action over online harassment, and would prefer to focus on the many вАЬloving messagesвАЭ that have been pouring in. The performer been getting thousands of messages daily, she told The Associated Press, most of them positive but some that she described as вАЬviolentвАЭ and even вАЬfrom the Middle Ages.вАЭ

Still, there are no misgivings, despite the backlash. Paloma said she was proud to have been part of a show that did not rely on a series of French cliches вАФ for example, вАЬthe Parisian with a baguette under their arm.вАЭ

вАЬIt could have been a postcard from 1930,вАЭ she said of the ceremony. вАЬBut instead, it was a photograph of France in 2024.вАЭ

Many agreed and praised the ceremony for its creativity, style and showmanship.

But French Catholic bishops and others were among those who said Christians had been offended, thoughParis Olympics organizers have saidthere was вАЬnever an intention to show disrespect to any religious groupвАЭ but rather to вАЬcelebrate community tolerance.вАЭ

Trump was asked on Fox News what he thought of the so-called вАЬLast SupperвАЭ scene. вАЬIвАЩm very open-minded,вАЭ the former president and current Republican nominee told host Laura Ingraham, вАЬbut I thought what they did was a disgrace.вАЭ

Of TrumpвАЩs comments, Paloma said: вАЬMy first reaction is to say that if Donald Trump is not reacting, then we have not done our job."

The criticism, she said, has been fueled by hate. вАЬWhere is the Catholicism, the Christianity in that? It is very hypocritical that their message is not about religion or kindness, itвАЩs about hate towards Jews, fat people, queer people and trans people.вАЭ

вАЬWe have been accused of trying to impose our vision on the world,вАЭ Bardin said. вАЬWe are not. ... We just want to let people know that we have a place in the world, and we are claiming that place.вАЭ

Paloma spoke to the AP in a phone interview and later at her Paris workshop, a studio devoted to her drag performance. Bardin debuted the drag queen persona some five years ago, the Spanish name inspired by the films of Pedro Almod√≥var.

Asked if she had any regrets, Paloma replied: вАЬMy only regrets is people's reactions. IвАЩm sorry if people are offended, but we did not try to parody, to mock вАШThe Last Supper.вАЩ It was not the point. So I can't regret what I did. IвАЩm sorry for people to only see things in a bad way.вАЭ

She added: вАЬMaybe change the perspective. Change the point of view. Try to see the beauty in what we did. Because it was just beauty. It was just only about beauty and reunion, and reparation.вАЭ

___

AP journalists Nicolas Garriga and Amira Borders in Paris contributed to this report.

___

For more coverage of the Paris Olympics, visithttps://apnews.com/hub/2024-paris-olympic-games.

title: Drag queen in Olympic opening ceremony has no regrets, calls it вАШa photograph of France in 2024вАЩ
author: Jocelyn Noveck, Associated Press
description: As a gay youth growing up in France, Hugo Bardin never felt he lived in a world that represented who he was.
